Black Crappie.about: A popular panfish prized for its tasty white flesh and schooling habits.
Black Crappie.identification: Deep, flattened silvery body with irregular black speckles and 7-8 dorsal spines.
Black Crappie.habitat: Clear, weedy lakes, ponds, and slow rivers with submerged cover.
Black Crappie.diet: Eats minnows and insects; best caught on small jigs, minnows, and tube baits.
Black Crappie.bestTime: Spring spawn is prime; fish dawn and dusk year-round.

Blue Catfish.about: The largest North American catfish, a hard-fighting big-river predator.
Blue Catfish.identification: Slate-blue body, deeply forked tail, and a straight-edged anal fin.
Blue Catfish.habitat: Deep channels and pools of large rivers and reservoirs.
Blue Catfish.diet: Eats fish and crustaceans; target with cut bait, shad, and skipjack.
Blue Catfish.bestTime: Summer and fall nights, especially in deeper holes.

Bluegill.about: An abundant, scrappy sunfish and a classic first catch for anglers.
Bluegill.identification: Round body with a black ear flap, dark vertical bars, and orange breast.
Bluegill.habitat: Weedy shallows of ponds, lakes, and slow streams.
Bluegill.diet: Eats insects and larvae; catch on worms, crickets, and small jigs.
Bluegill.bestTime: Late spring spawn near beds; mornings and evenings in summer.

Brook Trout.about: A small, colorful native char of cold, clean headwater streams.
Brook Trout.identification: Dark body with worm-like markings, red spots in blue halos, white-edged fins.
Brook Trout.habitat: Cold, clear, well-oxygenated mountain streams and northern lakes.
Brook Trout.diet: Eats insects and small fish; use flies, spinners, and worms.
Brook Trout.bestTime: Spring and fall in cool water; early morning best.

Brown Trout.about: A wary, hard-fighting trout introduced widely across North America.
Brown Trout.identification: Golden-brown body with black and red spots ringed by pale halos.
Brown Trout.habitat: Cool rivers, streams, and lakes with cover and current.
Brown Trout.diet: Eats insects, baitfish, and crayfish; use flies, spinners, and minnows.
Brown Trout.bestTime: Low light and overcast days in spring and fall.

Bullhead.about: Small, hardy bottom-feeding catfish common in murky waters.
Bullhead.identification: Squarish tail, rounded body, dark coloring, and chin barbels.
Bullhead.habitat: Mud-bottomed ponds, lakes, and sluggish rivers.
Bullhead.diet: Eats almost anything; catch on worms, stinkbait, and cut bait.
Bullhead.bestTime: Warm summer nights along the bottom.

Chain Pickerel.about: A toothy, aggressive ambush predator and miniature relative of the pike.
Chain Pickerel.identification: Greenish body with a distinct dark chain-like pattern and duck-bill snout.
Chain Pickerel.habitat: Weedy, slow backwaters of lakes, ponds, and rivers.
Chain Pickerel.diet: Eats fish and frogs; target with spinners, spoons, and live minnows.
Chain Pickerel.bestTime: Cooler water of fall and spring, midday in weeds.

Channel Catfish.about: The most widespread and popular catfish among North American anglers.
Channel Catfish.identification: Slender gray-olive body, deeply forked tail, and scattered dark spots.
Channel Catfish.habitat: Rivers, lakes, and ponds with sand, gravel, or rock bottoms.
Channel Catfish.diet: Eats fish, insects, and crayfish; use cut bait, nightcrawlers, and stinkbait.
Channel Catfish.bestTime: Summer evenings and nights near the bottom.

Chinook Salmon.about: The largest Pacific salmon, also called king salmon, a powerful sportfish.
Chinook Salmon.identification: Silver body with black spots on back and tail, and a black mouth with dark gums.
Chinook Salmon.habitat: Ocean and the Great Lakes, spawning in cold rivers and tributaries.
Chinook Salmon.diet: Eats baitfish; troll with spoons, plugs, and cut bait.
Chinook Salmon.bestTime: Late summer and fall runs as they stage near rivers.

Coho Salmon.about: A fast, acrobatic Pacific salmon also called silver salmon.
Coho Salmon.identification: Silver sides, spots only on upper tail lobe, black mouth with white gums.
Coho Salmon.habitat: Ocean and the Great Lakes, spawning in coastal rivers and streams.
Coho Salmon.diet: Eats baitfish and squid; troll spoons, flies, and trolling lures.
Coho Salmon.bestTime: Fall runs in rivers; morning and evening in lakes.

Crappie.about: A favorite schooling panfish covering both black and white crappie.
Crappie.identification: Deep, laterally flattened silvery body with dark speckles or bars.
Crappie.habitat: Weedy lakes, ponds, and slow rivers with brush and cover.
Crappie.diet: Eats minnows and insects; use small jigs, minnows, and tube baits.
Crappie.bestTime: Spring spawn near cover; dawn and dusk otherwise.

Flathead Catfish.about: A large solitary predator catfish that prefers live prey.
Flathead Catfish.identification: Flattened head, mottled yellow-brown body, and a squared tail with a protruding lower jaw.
Flathead Catfish.habitat: Deep pools, logjams, and cover in large rivers and reservoirs.
Flathead Catfish.diet: Eats live fish; target with live bluegill, shad, and sunfish.
Flathead Catfish.bestTime: Summer nights near submerged wood and cover.

Hybrid Striped Bass.about: A stocked cross of striped and white bass, hard-fighting and fast-growing.
Hybrid Striped Bass.identification: Silver body with broken, interrupted dark horizontal stripes and a deep body.
Hybrid Striped Bass.habitat: Open water of reservoirs and large rivers, often in schools.
Hybrid Striped Bass.diet: Eats shad and baitfish; use jigs, spoons, and live shad.
Hybrid Striped Bass.bestTime: Spring and fall when chasing schools of baitfish.

Lake Sturgeon.about: An ancient, long-lived bottom-dwelling giant with bony plates.
Lake Sturgeon.identification: Long body with rows of bony scutes, a shovel snout, and four barbels.
Lake Sturgeon.habitat: Deep channels and bottoms of large rivers and lakes.
Lake Sturgeon.diet: Eats insects, crustaceans, and mollusks; use nightcrawlers on bottom.
Lake Sturgeon.bestTime: Spring and early summer, often during regulated seasons.

Lake Trout.about: A large, deep-dwelling char of cold northern lakes.
Lake Trout.identification: Dark gray-green body with pale spots and a deeply forked tail.
Lake Trout.habitat: Deep, cold, well-oxygenated lakes, often well below the surface.
Lake Trout.diet: Eats baitfish; troll spoons, plugs, and jig with cut bait.
Lake Trout.bestTime: Spring and fall in shallows; deep trolling in summer.

Largemouth Bass.about: The most sought-after freshwater gamefish in North America.
Largemouth Bass.identification: Green body with a dark lateral stripe and jaw extending past the eye.
Largemouth Bass.habitat: Weedy, warm lakes, ponds, and slow rivers with cover.
Largemouth Bass.diet: Eats fish, frogs, and crayfish; use plastic worms, crankbaits, and spinnerbaits.
Largemouth Bass.bestTime: Spring spawn and warm mornings and evenings.

Muskellunge.about: The elusive apex predator known as the fish of ten thousand casts.
Muskellunge.identification: Long body with dark bars or spots on light background and forked tail.
Muskellunge.habitat: Weedy bays and rocky structure in large lakes and rivers.
Muskellunge.diet: Eats large fish; use big bucktails, jerkbaits, and crankbaits.
Muskellunge.bestTime: Fall is prime; low-light periods through warm months.

Northern Pike.about: An aggressive, toothy predator widespread across northern waters.
Northern Pike.identification: Greenish body with light bean-shaped spots and a duck-bill snout.
Northern Pike.habitat: Weedy shallows and cool waters of lakes and rivers.
Northern Pike.diet: Eats fish; target with spoons, spinnerbaits, and live minnows.
Northern Pike.bestTime: Spring and fall in cool water during daylight.

Oscar.about: A hardy South American cichlid established as an exotic in warm waters.
Oscar.identification: Dark oval body with orange mottling and an eyespot near the tail base.
Oscar.habitat: Warm canals, ponds, and lakes in tropical climates like Hawaii and Florida.
Oscar.diet: Eats fish and invertebrates; use small jigs, worms, and live bait.
Oscar.bestTime: Warm daylight hours near vegetation and structure.

Peacock Bass.about: A hard-hitting tropical cichlid prized as an exotic sportfish.
Peacock Bass.identification: Golden-green body with dark vertical bars and an eyespot on the tail.
Peacock Bass.habitat: Warm canals, lakes, and ponds in tropical regions like Hawaii and Florida.
Peacock Bass.diet: Eats fish; target with crankbaits, jigs, and live shiners.
Peacock Bass.bestTime: Warm sunny midday hours near cover.

Rainbow Trout.about: A widely stocked, acrobatic trout popular with anglers everywhere.
Rainbow Trout.identification: Silvery body with black spots and a pink to red lateral stripe.
Rainbow Trout.habitat: Cold, clear streams, rivers, and lakes with good oxygen.
Rainbow Trout.diet: Eats insects and small fish; use flies, spinners, and PowerBait.
Rainbow Trout.bestTime: Spring and fall; mornings and evenings in cool water.

Redear Sunfish.about: A larger sunfish nicknamed shellcracker for its snail-eating habits.
Redear Sunfish.identification: Olive body with a bright red or orange margin on the ear flap.
Redear Sunfish.habitat: Warm ponds and lakes with vegetation and firm bottoms.
Redear Sunfish.diet: Eats snails and invertebrates; use worms and small bottom baits.
Redear Sunfish.bestTime: Spring spawn near beds in shallow water.

Sauger.about: A river-loving cousin of the walleye built for turbid current.
Sauger.identification: Brassy body with distinct dark blotches and spotted dorsal fins.
Sauger.habitat: Turbid, fast pools and tailwaters of large rivers.
Sauger.diet: Eats small fish and invertebrates; use jigs, minnows, and crankbaits.
Sauger.bestTime: Late fall and winter near dams and current breaks.

Smallmouth Bass.about: A hard-fighting bass renowned for explosive jumps and strength.
Smallmouth Bass.identification: Bronze body with dark vertical bars and a jaw ending at the eye.
Smallmouth Bass.habitat: Cool, clear rocky rivers and lakes with gravel and current.
Smallmouth Bass.diet: Eats crayfish and minnows; use tube jigs, crankbaits, and soft plastics.
Smallmouth Bass.bestTime: Late spring and fall; mornings around rocky structure.

Spotted Bass.about: A scrappy bass of clear streams, often confused with largemouth.
Spotted Bass.identification: Green body with a lateral stripe of diamond blotches and rows of belly spots.
Spotted Bass.habitat: Clear, rocky streams and reservoirs with current and cover.
Spotted Bass.diet: Eats crayfish and minnows; use jigs, crankbaits, and soft plastics.
Spotted Bass.bestTime: Spring and fall near rocky points and drop-offs.

Striped Bass.about: A powerful migratory gamefish thriving in coastal and inland waters.
Striped Bass.identification: Silver body with seven to eight distinct unbroken horizontal stripes.
Striped Bass.habitat: Open water of large reservoirs, rivers, and coastal estuaries.
Striped Bass.diet: Eats shad and herring; troll or cast spoons, swimbaits, and live bait.
Striped Bass.bestTime: Spring spawn runs and fall when baitfish school.

Walleye.about: A prized table fish named for its large, light-gathering glassy eyes.
Walleye.identification: Olive-gold body with a white-tipped tail and milky reflective eyes.
Walleye.habitat: Cool lakes and rivers with rocky or sandy bottoms.
Walleye.diet: Eats small fish; use jigs, minnows, crawler harnesses, and crankbaits.
Walleye.bestTime: Dawn, dusk, and overcast days; spring and fall peak.

White Bass.about: A fast schooling fish that drives aggressive feeding frenzies.
White Bass.identification: Silver body with several thin dark stripes and a deep compressed body.
White Bass.habitat: Open water of large lakes, reservoirs, and rivers.
White Bass.diet: Eats shad and minnows; use jigs, spoons, and small crankbaits.
White Bass.bestTime: Spring spawning runs up rivers and surface feeding at dawn.

White Crappie.about: A schooling panfish tolerant of murkier water than its black cousin.
White Crappie.identification: Silvery body with faint vertical bars and 5-6 dorsal spines.
White Crappie.habitat: Turbid lakes, reservoirs, and slow rivers with brush.
White Crappie.diet: Eats minnows and insects; use small jigs, minnows, and tube baits.
White Crappie.bestTime: Spring spawn near cover; dawn and dusk in warm months.

White Sturgeon.about: North America's largest freshwater fish, an ancient long-lived giant.
White Sturgeon.identification: Long gray body with rows of bony scutes, a blunt snout, and four barbels.
White Sturgeon.habitat: Deep pools and channels of large western rivers and estuaries.
White Sturgeon.diet: Eats fish, eggs, and invertebrates; use cut bait and roe on bottom.
White Sturgeon.bestTime: Spring and fall on the bottom during regulated seasons.

Yellow Perch.about: A tasty, abundant schooling panfish popular for year-round angling.
Yellow Perch.identification: Golden-yellow body with six to eight dark vertical bars and orange fins.
Yellow Perch.habitat: Weedy lakes, ponds, and slow rivers with cooler water.
Yellow Perch.diet: Eats insects and minnows; use small jigs, worms, and minnows.
Yellow Perch.bestTime: Daytime year-round; excellent through the ice in winter.
